# **LiDAR-Derived Relief Visualisations for Archaeological Prospection: Algorithmic Foundations and QGIS Plugin Engineering**

Airborne Laser Scanning (ALS), commonly referred to as LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging), has initiated a paradigm shift within the discipline of archaeological prospection. By emitting high-frequency laser pulses and measuring their return times, LiDAR sensors can penetrate dense vegetative canopies—such as tropical rainforests or temperate deciduous woodlands—to generate highly accurate, high-resolution Digital Elevation Models (DEMs) of the bare earth. These DEMs inherently contain the subtle geometric signatures of past anthropogenic activities, capturing micro-topographic anomalies that range from the faint convexities of medieval ridge-and-furrow agriculture to the massive concave earthworks of prehistoric enclosures. However, raw DEM data is natively difficult for the human optical system to interpret. The standard method of representation—analytical hillshading—assumes a single directional light source. While computationally inexpensive, analytical hillshading introduces severe directional biases; archaeological features lying parallel to the illumination azimuth are frequently obscured by shadows or washed out by direct light.  
To overcome the fundamental limitations of analytical hillshading, the geomorphometric and archaeological communities have engineered a suite of specialized relief visualisation algorithms. These techniques mathematically filter, detrend, and illuminate the terrain to enhance localized micro-relief independently of macro-topographic gradients and directional lighting. The engineering of a Quantum GIS (QGIS) processing plugin dedicated to computing these advanced visualisations requires a rigorous synthesis of geospatial mathematics, spatial software architecture, array-based computational mechanics, and increasingly, the integration of deep learning paradigms. This comprehensive report delineates the theoretical and mathematical frameworks of advanced relief visualisations, the mechanical implementation of a QGIS 3.x processing plugin utilizing the Geospatial Data Abstraction Library (GDAL) and NumPy, and the future integration trajectories for automated feature extraction using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs).

## **The Relief Visualization Toolbox (RVT): Architecture and Licensing**

The Relief Visualization Toolbox (RVT) is universally acknowledged as the definitive computational framework for processing high-resolution elevation models in archaeological contexts. Originally developed as a standalone application, RVT has since evolved to offer its core computational methodologies as an open-source Python library (rvt\_py), empowering scientists and software engineers to visualize raster elevation datasets and natively integrate these algorithms into secondary applications and graphical user interfaces.1  
The architectural philosophy of the RVT Python library emphasizes strict modularity to facilitate integration into broader geoprocessing pipelines. The source code, which is publicly hosted on GitHub (EarthObservation/RVT\_py), is partitioned into three primary operational modules.2 The rvt.vis module encompasses the core mathematical functions responsible for executing the transformations on multi-dimensional matrices, covering operations such as sky-view factor, openness, and simple local relief models.3 The rvt.blend module provides sophisticated algorithmic fusion capabilities, allowing discrete visualisations to be mathematically merged using blending modes—such as multiply, screen, or overlay—to create highly analytical composites.3 Finally, the rvt.default module manages default parameterization, execution logic, and I/O handling, providing a standardized interface for processing raster inputs and managing memory efficiently.3  
Crucially for the development of an independent QGIS processing plugin, the RVT Python library is released under the permissive Apache License Version 2.0.3 Unlike restrictive "copyleft" licenses, the Apache 2.0 license grants expansive permissions for commercial use, modification, and the distribution of derivative works. The primary legal requirements dictate that derivative software must include the original copyright notice, provide a copy of the Apache 2.0 license, state any significant modifications made to the original files, and include a disclaimer of warranty.5 Therefore, directly embedding rvt\_py as a bundled dependency within a custom QGIS Processing plugin is both legally supported and highly recommended to prevent the reinvention of complex mathematical filtering operations.

## **Mathematical Foundations of Advanced Relief Algorithms**

The core of modern archaeological topographical analysis relies on algorithms that isolate specific geometric properties of a DEM. These methods operate by statistically evaluating a moving neighborhood—frequently termed a focal window or kernel—around each individual raster cell to quantify its relative exposure, elevation deviation, or angular dominance compared to its immediate terrain context.

### **Sky-View Factor (SVF)**

The Sky-View Factor (SVF) was originally conceptualized within urban climatology to calculate radiative heat loss, but it has been successfully adapted for geomorphometry as a diffuse illumination metric. SVF quantifies the proportion of the overlying celestial hemisphere that is visible from a specific observation point on the terrain surface.6 Unlike analytical hillshading, which assumes a single directional light source and can obscure features, SVF utilizes a fictive light source that illuminates the relief surface uniformly from the entire celestial hemisphere centered at the point being evaluated, effectively neglecting the Earth's curvature over short distances and ignoring any direction below the mathematical horizon.6  
Mathematically, the algorithm computes the maximum vertical elevation angle of the horizon (![][image1]) along ![][image2] discrete azimuthal search directions, restricted by a maximum search radius (![][image3]).8 The vertical angle is defined geometrically as ![][image4], where ![][image5] denotes the maximum elevation difference and ![][image6] denotes the horizontal distance from the origin pixel to a given point along the search vector.9 The total SVF is then evaluated by integrating these maximum elevation angles over the hemisphere:  
![][image7]  
The resulting values of SVF range continuously from ![][image8] to ![][image9]. An SVF approaching ![][image9] signifies nearly complete visibility of the sky hemisphere, which is characteristic of prominent peaks, ridges, or the apices of burial mounds.9 Conversely, values approaching ![][image8] indicate that the sky is highly obscured by surrounding terrain, representing deep, confined depressions such as ditches, sunken roads, or mining pits.11  
Computationally, the horizon scanning algorithm requires casting multiple rays across the gridded DEM for every single pixel. Because calculating the line-of-sight for millions of pixels is an exceptionally intensive operation, typical Python implementations using NumPy utilize optimized matrix shifting or localized array slicing to approximate the ray traces without resorting to nested iterative loops.12 However, as the spatial resolution of LiDAR datasets increases, these CPU-bound NumPy approximations encounter severe performance bottlenecks. To render SVF calculation tractable on massive regional datasets, cutting-edge implementations have increasingly migrated toward GPU acceleration. Frameworks such as CuPy or customized CUDA kernels can calculate the independent per-cell ray traces via highly parallelized threads.14 Models like SOLWEIG-GPU leverage these auto-casted GPU mathematical operations and map-overlap tiling to process massive datasets (e.g., exceeding 100 million cells) exponentially faster than standard CPU libraries.14

### **Topographic Openness**

Openness expands upon the foundational concepts of SVF by extending the calculation from the celestial hemisphere to a full spherical perspective. While SVF artificially truncates calculations at the horizontal plane, Openness evaluates the angular relationships of the terrain irrespective of the mathematical horizon.18 It is mathematically partitioned into two discrete, complementary metrics: Positive Openness and Negative Openness.11  
Positive Openness evaluates the mean zenith angle along multiple radial search vectors. It heavily emphasizes convex topographies, making it an exceptional tool for identifying ridges, ramparts, surviving wall foundations, and the earthen boundaries of enclosures.18 Conversely, Negative Openness evaluates the mean nadir angle (the angle pointing straight down into the terrain). By measuring the angular depth of the surrounding landscape, Negative Openness heavily emphasizes concave topographies, illuminating ancient ditches, hollow ways, defensive moats, and sunken building platforms.21  
The mathematical formulation expresses these metrics as the mean of the observed angles over ![][image2] directions:  
![][image10]  
![][image11]  
Because Openness evaluates the full sphere, it produces a visualization that is inherently detrended. It effectively normalizes the macro-topography, rendering local micro-relief variations equally visible regardless of whether they are situated on steep mountainous slopes or completely flat alluvial plains.19 This results in an image that initially appears "flatter" than SVF but contains significantly more pronounced local terrain features.19

### **Multi-Scale Topographic Position (MSTP)**

Traditional focal statistics, such as standard openness or localized slope, are highly scale-dependent. An algorithmic radius optimized to highlight a small 5-meter burial mound will frequently smooth over and completely obscure a massive 50-meter hillfort enclosure.24 Multi-Scale Topographic Position (MSTP) was developed to circumvent this exact limitation by calculating the Deviation from Mean Elevation (DEV) across a continuous range of spatial scales simultaneously.21  
DEV is a unitless measure of topographic position. It is calculated as the difference between the elevation of the central pixel (![][image12]) and the mean elevation of the roving window (![][image13]), which is then normalized by a ruggedness metric or local standard deviation (![][image14]).27  
![][image15]  
Positive values of DEV indicate convex or locally elevated areas, while negative values indicate concave or locally depressed areas.28 To compute this efficiently without causing exponential increases in processing time as the window radii grow to hundreds of pixels, MSTP utilizes an "integral image" (or summed-area table) approach. This data structure allows the sum of values within any rectangular neighborhood to be computed in constant mathematical time, requiring only four array lookups regardless of the vast size of the focal window.24  
MSTP evaluates DEV at three distinct scale ranges: local, meso, and broad.26 The algorithm then compiles the maximum deviations from these three ranges into a composite ternary RGB image.26 Typically, the Red band is mapped to the broad-scale positioning, the Green band is mapped to the meso-scale positioning, and the Blue band is allocated to isolate the local micro-scale features.26 The resulting false-color composite immediately informs the archaeologist of the nested nature of the terrain, showcasing exactly where small features exist within larger geomorphological contexts.25  
Recent algorithmic advancements within the RVT framework have produced enhanced variants of this technique, explicitly designed for archaeological prospection, known as e3MSTP and e4MSTP.2 The e4MSTP variant fuses multi-scale terrain representations with highly localized morphological data.31 It programmatically blends a red-toned slope visualization, combined Positive and Negative Openness, Local Dominance, and SVF evaluated at dual radii with specialized histogram stretches, all overlaid onto the standard MSTP base.31 This extreme, complex blending technique enables the simultaneous detection of massive defensive earthworks and highly subtle structural remains, performing exceptionally well in hyper-flat environments where standard techniques fail to provide sufficient contrast.24

### **Simple Local Relief Model (SLRM)**

The Simple Local Relief Model (SLRM) operates strictly as a high-pass spatial filter that systematically strips away the macro-topographic trend of the landscape to leave behind only the localized elevation anomalies.33 This approach is exceptionally valuable in terrains characterized by rolling hills or steep gradients, where large-scale elevation changes overwhelm the visual perception of small archaeological structures.  
The algorithmic generation of an SLRM involves a multi-step sequence. First, the initial DEM is aggressively smoothed using a specified low-pass filter to generate a representation of the terrain's general trend.34 This smoothing operation is governed by a discrete radius parameter, which defines the threshold between what is considered "macro" trend and "micro" anomaly.34 The smoothed surface matrix is then directly subtracted from the original high-resolution DEM matrix.34  
![][image16]  
The output of this matrix subtraction is a detrended raster where the ![][image8] value corresponds precisely to the localized mean ground level.35 Positive floating-point values represent relative height above the localized ground level (indicating embankments, walls, or mounds), while negative floating-point values represent relative depth below the localized ground level (indicating pits, moats, or sunken features).35 Because the SLRM is entirely dependent on its singular radius parameter, archaeologists must iteratively test different radii to successfully isolate the specific wavelength of the target archaeological feature class.37

### **Local Dominance**

Local Dominance operates on the principle of angular pixel dominance and provides an alternative method for determining whether the morphology of a specific region exhibits convex or concave characteristics.34 It conceptually simulates a human observer standing on a specific focal pixel and evaluates how predominantly that observer overlooks the surrounding terrain within a specified radial distance.23  
The algorithm determines the average angle at which the hypothetical observer looks down upon or up toward the local surface.23 High Local Dominance values signify a wide angle of visibility from the central pixel, meaning the terrain drops away rapidly; this characterizes prominent peaks, ridges, or the apices of burial mounds.34 Low values signify that the surrounding terrain rises above the pixel, which characterizes depressions, ravines, or the interior bottoms of defensive ditches.34 Local Dominance is conceptually aligned with the SLRM and is highly complementary, but it retains a slightly stronger visual impression of the broader landscape context, as pixels situated on steep slopes output higher baseline dominance values than pixels situated on completely horizontal plains.40

| Algorithm | Mathematical Principle | Primary Function | Ideal Geometric Targets |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Sky-View Factor (SVF)** | Diffuse hemispherical illumination. | Identifies proportion of visible sky; measures relative depression. | Concave features, pits, ditches, sunken paths. |
| **Positive Openness** | Mean zenith angle. | Emphasizes convexity irrespective of macro-slope. | Ridges, ramparts, earthen walls, barrow crests. |
| **Negative Openness** | Mean nadir angle. | Emphasizes concavity irrespective of macro-slope. | Moats, structural depressions, hollow ways. |
| **MSTP** | Deviation from Mean Elevation (DEV) across scales. | Isolates nested topographic features across local, meso, and broad radii. | Complex multi-scalar landscapes, massive hillforts. |
| **SLRM** | Subtraction of low-pass smoothed DEM from original DEM. | High-pass detrending; isolates local relative elevation. | Shallow burial mounds, extremely subtle foundations. |
| **Local Dominance** | Angular dominance of a central pixel over its radius. | Evaluates terrain convexity/concavity via simulated observer visibility. | Low-relief features in gentle topographies. |

## **Geomorphometric Signatures and Archaeological Use Cases**

Archaeological features manifest across diverse spatial extents and geometric configurations. The structural remnants of ancient populations are inevitably degraded by millennia of bioturbation, erosion, and modern agricultural practices. Consequently, a singular visualization technique is rarely sufficient to identify all temporal and functional site types within a given study area. The correct pairing of specific visualisation algorithms, scale parameters, and colour ramps is highly critical for successful interpretation.  
**Medieval Ridge-and-Furrow Systems** Medieval and post-medieval ridge-and-furrow agriculture is characterized by widespread, parallel, highly repetitive low-amplitude convex/concave undulations. Because these features cover massive areas with very low relative relief, they are best detected using Positive and Negative Openness, or Multi-directional Hillshade.11 A relatively small search radius of approximately ![][image17] ensures the algorithm captures the specific wavelength of the furrows without smoothing them into the broader terrain. High-contrast grayscale mapping or divergent color ramps (where crests map to red and troughs map to blue) are highly recommended to highlight the repetitive linearity.11  
**Prehistoric Barrows and Burial Mounds** Prehistoric burial mounds manifest as isolated, highly localized convex protrusions. Depending on their preservation, they are often heavily eroded, surviving only a few centimeters above the surrounding topography. The Simple Local Relief Model (SLRM) and Local Dominance are unparalleled in isolating these geometries.1 The SLRM trend assessment radius must be calibrated strictly to the maximum expected diameter of the barrow (typically ![][image18] depending on spatial resolution).1 The output should be visualized using a standardized, symmetric color stretch (e.g., locking the minimum to ![][image19] and the maximum to ![][image20]) utilizing a saturated-to-desaturated heat ramp, forcing the subtle convexities to "pop" aggressively against the ![][image8]\-value background.35  
**Roman Road Aggers** Roman road infrastructure is frequently characterized by the agger—a linear, continuous convex earthen ridge that traverses the macro-topography for kilometers, often accompanied by parallel side-ditches. Multi-Scale Topographic Position (MSTP) combined with Positive Openness provides the optimal analytical environment.18 MSTP allows the archaeologist to retain the long-distance macro-context of the route while simultaneously detecting the highly localized linear variation of the raised agger.26 RGB composite mapping of the MSTP scales isolates the continuous artificial corridor effectively against surrounding natural geological patterns.26  
**Enclosure Ditches and Moats** Prehistoric and historic defensive enclosures are defined by their boundary ditches, which present as subdued, concave, negative linear anomalies. The Sky-View Factor (SVF) and Negative Openness are the premier algorithms for detecting these specific excavations.20 An SVF search radius of ![][image21], evaluated across ![][image22] to ![][image23] discrete directions, ensures optimal detection.21 These results are best visualized using a high-contrast inverted grayscale ramp. By inverting the standard representation—so that low SVF values approach true black—the optical illusion of depth is forced upon the viewer, causing the ditches to stand out prominently as dark, geometric scars on the landscape.44  
**Charcoal Kilns and Production Platforms** Relict charcoal hearths and production kilns represent specialized industrial features, commonly presenting as small, dense, highly circular depressions, or as artificially terraced flat platforms cut directly into steep mountain slopes. The SVF and the highly complex e4MSTP are optimal for these environments.44 A tight radius of ![][image24] ensures the algorithm captures the specific footprint of the kiln.44 SVF is particularly adept at isolating unnatural flat terracing against the broader trend of a steep slope, appearing as bright circular anomalies on otherwise darkened terrain.44  
**Tropical Site Vegetation and Plazuela Groups** The identification of complex ancient architectural footprints, such as Maya plazuela groups, is exceptionally difficult when they are located beneath heavy tropical forest canopies and intermixed with natural karst sinkholes and extreme limestone erosion. The SLRM and the Visualization for Archaeological Topography (VAT) algorithm provide the highest detection rates.21 VAT operates by blending slope, Openness, and SVF into a unified image that emphasizes architectural sharp edges.21 Simultaneously, the SLRM aggressively removes the rolling limestone karst topography, exposing the highly geometric, anthropogenic nature of the plazuela platforms that would otherwise be lost in the geological noise.44  
Color manipulation is just as critical as algorithmic selection. Utilizing simple linear min-max stretches is actively discouraged in archaeological visualization, as extreme outliers (such as deep modern quarries or sharp modern road embankments) will completely collapse the optical range available for the ancient earthworks.49 Instead, applying Standard Deviations (typically clipping at ![][image25] to ![][image26] standard deviations) forces the GIS software to stretch the color ramp aggressively over the central statistical cluster of the data, which is precisely where the highly subtle archaeological deviations reside.46

## **Engineering the QGIS 3.x Processing Plugin Architecture**

Integrating complex, array-based geomorphometry into Quantum GIS relies entirely on the QGIS 3.x Processing Framework. Historically, plugins were built as standalone dock widgets executing isolated Python scripts. However, modern architecture dictates that algorithms be developed as fully registered geoprocessing tools. By subclassing the core Processing API, the custom plugin inherently gains access to QGIS’s parallel batch-processing interfaces, the graphical modeler, background task threading, and robust error-handling mechanisms without requiring the developer to build complex, bespoke graphical user interfaces.50

### **Plugin Directory Structure and Initialization**

A standard QGIS Processing plugin necessitates a strict hierarchical directory structure to be correctly recognized and initialized by the QGIS Python environment.50

| File / Directory Name | Structural Purpose in the QGIS Framework |
| :---- | :---- |
| metadata.txt | Defines core metadata. Must explicitly include the flag hasProcessingProvider=yes to initialize the processing architecture.50 |
| \_\_init\_\_.py | The package initialization file utilizing the classFactory() method to pass the QGIS interface reference to the plugin.51 |
| plugin\_main.py | Contains the initGui() and unload() functions, responsible for invoking QgsApplication.processingRegistry().addProvider().50 |
| processing\_provider/ | A designated sub-package housing the specific algorithmic provider and the individual tools.50 |
| ... provider.py | Subclasses QgsProcessingProvider. Exposes the plugin’s algorithms to the QGIS registry via loadAlgorithms().50 |
| ... algorithm.py | Subclasses QgsProcessingAlgorithm. Contains the parameter definitions, logic, and NumPy/GDAL integration.50 |

The main initialization file manages the lifecycle of the provider. When the plugin is activated, it instantiates the provider class and registers it with the global QGIS processing registry. When deactivated, it gracefully removes the provider, ensuring no memory leaks or orphaned interface elements remain.50 The provider class itself serves to group the algorithms under a unified banner within the Processing Toolbox, dictating the human-friendly name and the designated icon for the category.50

### **Core Classes of the Processing API**

The operational engine of the plugin is the specific Python script extending the QgsProcessingAlgorithm class.53 This class enforces a strict lifecycle comprising multiple mandatory methods that dictate how the tool interacts with the user and the underlying data.  
**The initAlgorithm() Method** This method is strictly reserved for declaring the required input parameters and output destinations. To process LiDAR DEMs, the plugin defines inputs utilizing the QgsProcessingParameterRasterLayer class. This specific parameter automatically populates a dropdown menu in the auto-generated GUI with all raster files currently loaded in the QGIS layer tree, preventing the user from manually typing file paths.53 Numeric parameters representing mathematical variables, such as search radii or angular bounds, are declared via QgsProcessingParameterNumber, complete with hardcoded boundary limits, data types (integers or doubles), and logical default values.57 Crucially, outputs must be strictly declared utilizing the QgsProcessingParameterRasterDestination class, which guarantees that the resulting raster is accurately routed to the user's desired directory or handled dynamically by the QGIS temporary file structure.56  
**The processAlgorithm() Method** When the user executes the tool, QGIS invokes the processAlgorithm(self, parameters, context, feedback) method. The developer must first retrieve the raw inputs. The layer URIs are extracted using self.parameterAsRasterLayer(), user-defined floats are extracted via self.parameterAsDouble(), and the designated output path is acquired using self.parameterAsOutputLayer().59  
During this execution phase, the feedback object is highly critical. Complex horizon scanning algorithms processing billions of pixels may take significant time. The feedback object allows the algorithm to push textual updates (feedback.setProgressText()) and percentage updates to the GUI's progress bar (feedback.setProgress()).52 Furthermore, the algorithm must iteratively check feedback.isCanceled(); if the user triggers an interruption, the script must immediately halt processing and release memory to prevent a hard crash of the main QGIS thread.52

### **Raster Processing Paradigms Using GDAL and NumPy**

Because QGIS raster layer objects are complex GUI abstractions, they cannot be directly processed by mathematical algebraic algorithms. The plugin must establish a rigorous interface between the QGIS layer objects, the Geospatial Data Abstraction Library (GDAL), and NumPy multi-dimensional arrays.  
The standard processing pattern initiates by extracting the underlying system file path from the QGIS raster object using provider.dataSourceUri().59 GDAL is then utilized to load the dataset into memory via gdal.Open(). The specific elevation band (usually band 1 for DEMs) is extracted via GetRasterBand(1) and subsequently cast into a multidimensional NumPy array using the ReadAsArray() method.59  
**Handling NoData and Coordinate Reference Systems (CRS)** Geospatial LiDAR arrays frequently contain specific 'NoData' values (e.g., ![][image27] or ![][image28]) representing the irregular edges of the scan boundaries. If left unaltered, SVF and MSTP focal windows will ingest these extreme mathematical outliers, causing catastrophic edge artifacting that ripples inward across the DEM.63 Within NumPy, these specific values must be located and mathematically masked using logical operations like np.where(), or directly converted to np.nan prior to processing, ensuring they are cleanly ignored by localized matrix operations.63  
Once the core visualization algorithm completes its mathematical transformation on the matrix, the resulting NumPy array must be carefully serialized back into a valid GeoTIFF file. A new GDAL driver must be invoked (gdal.GetDriverByName('GTiff')) to initialize the target output raster.65 Critically, to preserve the precise geospatial alignment of the original LiDAR DEM, the script must extract the affine transform matrix (which dictates the origin X, pixel width, rotation, origin Y, rotation, and pixel height) and the specific geographic projection string from the input dataset using GetGeoTransform() and GetProjection().62 These identical properties are then injected directly into the newly created output dataset using SetGeoTransform() and SetProjection().63  
Finally, the modified NumPy array is burned into the output raster using the WriteArray() method. The NoData value is formally re-established via SetNoDataValue(), and the FlushCache() method is called to ensure all physical disk writing is completed before QGIS is instructed to load the resulting file onto the canvas.63

## **Navigating the QGIS Plugin Repository Submission Protocols**

To make the LiDAR visualization plugin globally accessible to the archaeological and geoinformatics communities, it must be successfully submitted and navigated through the official QGIS Plugin Repository approval process.68 This process is governed by strict rules designed to ensure stability, security, and open-source compliance.

1. **Account Authentication and Licensing:** The primary publisher must register for an official OSGeo ID to access the upload portal.68 The developed software must be explicitly licensed under the GNU General Public License Version 2 (GPLv2) or a highly compatible open-source license (such as Apache 2.0 or MIT) to conform seamlessly with the broader QGIS ecosystem.71  
2. **Metadata and Documentation Integrity:** The metadata.txt file must be comprehensively populated and structurally flawless. It strictly requires valid URLs pointing directly to the public source code repository (e.g., GitHub or GitLab), a designated issue tracker for bug reporting, and a dedicated homepage or highly descriptive README file explaining the plugin's core utility.68  
3. **External Dependency Management:** If the plugin architecture requires external Python libraries that are not natively bundled within the standard OSGeo4W Python environment (such as CuPy for GPU acceleration, or advanced SciPy modules), these dependencies must be explicitly detailed in the description field.71 The developer must provide executable, step-by-step instructions for users to install these dependencies across Windows, Linux, and macOS platforms.71  
4. **Strict Packaging Constraints:** The repository system requires a compressed .zip package containing only the operational Python code and UI assets.68 Hidden directories (.git, \_\_pycache\_\_), system-specific indexing files (\_\_MACOSX), and compiled binaries must be explicitly purged from the archive prior to upload, as their presence will trigger immediate automated rejection.68 The source code within the .zip archive will be heavily scrutinized against the linked public code repository to guarantee absolute equivalence.71 Furthermore, the total .zip package size must not exceed the strict 25MB architectural threshold.68

## **Deep Learning Paradigms for Automated Archaeological Detection**

While advanced algorithmic visualisations drastically enhance the visibility of micro-topographic archaeological features to the human optical system, the manual digitisation of LiDAR data across thousands of square kilometers remains a monumental, highly subjective bottleneck. The integration of Deep Learning paradigms—specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)—represents the next major evolutionary frontier for large-scale archaeological prospection. Crucially, this integration represents a highly viable future extension of the QGIS plugin architecture.

### **Current Research Architectures and Pre-processing**

The successful archaeological application of Deep Learning relies heavily on utilizing LiDAR-derived topographic algorithms as foundational inputs. Research definitively demonstrates that feeding raw elevation grids directly into a neural network produces exceptionally poor accuracy; the CNN becomes mathematically overwhelmed by macro-topographic slopes and massive geological formations.74 However, when the raw DEM is rigorously pre-processed into a Local Relief Model (LRM), MSTP, or an SVF composite, the macro-topographic trends are normalized.74 This effectively treats the visualisations as multi-spectral bands, allowing the convolutional filters within the neural network to focus purely on the geometric morphology of the archaeological anomalies.24  
State-of-the-art implementations predominantly utilize highly advanced semantic segmentation and object detection networks. Architectures such as U-Net, ResNet18, Mask R-CNN, and YOLO (You Only Look Once) have been successfully benchmarked for identifying specific anthropogenic features.45

* **U-Net and ResUnet:** These architectures excel in pixel-wise semantic segmentation and have been heavily applied to map highly complex, overlapping, and sprawling features, such as immense Celtic field systems, extensive agricultural terracing, and linear stone wall networks.47 U-Net variants tend to be highly conservative in their classifications, yielding very high Precision metrics but often suffering from lower Recall.77  
* **Mask R-CNN and YOLO:** These are object-detection frameworks designed to draw tightly fitting bounding boxes and instance masks around isolated, spatially distinct geometries. They have been deployed successfully to detect discreet anomalies like prehistoric burial mounds (barrows), historic post-mining pits, and charcoal kilns.47

### **Training Data Strategies and Accuracy Constraints**

The fundamental, pervasive limitation of deep learning in archaeological prospection is the severe scarcity of heavily annotated, highly accurate ground-truthed training data.45 Because archaeological structures exist within highly heterogeneous terrain environments—spanning steep mountainous terrain, dense shrubland, and varying modern land use—models suffer from excessively high False Positive rates.74 Convolutional networks commonly mistake natural geological outcrops, modern forestry interventions (such as logging tracks), or natural sinkholes for ancient earthworks due to morphological similarities.74  
To bypass this critical data scarcity, researchers have increasingly pioneered synthetic training approaches. By computationally generating and inserting simulated three-dimensional models of specific archaeological sites (e.g., procedural burial mounds or linear walls) into varied, real-world LiDAR background terrains, vast and diverse synthetic datasets can be generated to train CNNs without requiring years of manual digitisation.1 Additionally, Object-Based Image Analysis (OBIA) is frequently layered alongside Deep Neural Networks to cross-verify geometries, utilizing multi-modal data including historical aerial orthophotos fused with LiDAR SVF rasters to actively suppress false detections.47  
When networks are trained on optimally processed visualization composites (such as RVT's MSTP and SLRM base layers), model accuracies have reached remarkable levels. Researchers routinely report highly competitive Average Precision (AP) metrics, with certain tuned networks successfully automating over 80% of charcoal kiln and burial mound detection tasks across expansive regional test zones.45

### **Future Integration Potential within QGIS**

In future iterative upgrades, the proposed QGIS processing plugin could transcend mere visualization generation by functioning as a fully automated archaeological inference engine. By integrating standardized deep learning libraries such as PyTorch or the ONNX Runtime API directly into the plugin's Python environment, the plugin could execute a seamless pipeline. It would accept a raw LiDAR DEM, execute the SVF and SLRM processing automatically utilizing the optimal NumPy/GDAL routines, pass the resulting multi-band visualization matrices directly through an embedded, pre-trained U-Net tensor model, and output a fully georeferenced QGIS vector shapefile mapping the predicted archaeological structures.1 This seamless integration of deterministic mathematical filtering and probabilistic machine learning within a singular, open-source QGIS environment would radically accelerate the pace, scale, and accuracy of modern landscape archaeology.

#### **Works cited**

1. Using Simulated Training Data to Locate Archaeological Sites with Machine Learning \- Cambridge University Press & Assessment, accessed May 23, 2026, [https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/advances-in-archaeological-practice/article/using-simulated-training-data-to-locate-archaeological-sites-with-machine-learning/3C239F68BDB24A11F1DA0DFB8939F089](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/advances-in-archaeological-practice/article/using-simulated-training-data-to-locate-archaeological-sites-with-machine-learning/3C239F68BDB24A11F1DA0DFB8939F089)
